system_context.business_goals: Optimize plant maintenance, ensure safety, and improve efficiency and quality of simulation work.

architectures.name: WRCS Monolithic Revision Control Architecture
architectures.description: WRCS is a monolithic revision control system built on RCS with a layered structure. The architecture separates user interface concerns (visualization), core revision control logic, and utility functions. Components communicate through direct function calls in a tightly-coupled executable structure. The system was reverse-engineered from executables and libraries, indicating an organically-evolved rather than intentionally-designed architecture.
architectures.architecture_characteristics: Tightly-coupled monolithic design with moderate modularity. Limited scalability due to monolithic structure. High cohesion within functional areas (diff operations, display, reporting). The architecture prioritizes simplicity and direct integration over flexibility. Quality characteristics include: functional correctness for version control operations, moderate maintainability through functional separation, and limited extensibility due to tight coupling.

key_components.responsibilities: Primary entry point and orchestration layer. Coordinates overall workflow, processes user commands, and delegates to specialized components. Acts as the central controller for all WRCS operations including archive creation, file check-in/check-out, release management, and version rollback.
key_components.change_sensitivity: High - Changes here ripple throughout the system as this is the central orchestrator

key_components.responsibilities: Core revision control logic built on RCS. Manages archives, tracks file changes, handles version history, performs check-in/check-out operations, creates releases, and manages rollback to previous versions. This is the business logic heart of the system.
key_components.change_sensitivity: High - Core business logic; changes affect fundamental system behavior and data integrity

key_components.responsibilities: Performs file and version comparisons. Analyzes differences between file versions, generates delta information for change tracking, and provides comparison data for both storage optimization and user review.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Well-isolated utility function, but changes affect comparison accuracy across the system

key_components.responsibilities: Presentation layer for comparison results. Formats and displays file differences to users in a readable format. Handles user interface concerns for visualizing changes between versions.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low - UI component with clear boundary; changes primarily affect presentation, not core functionality

key_components.responsibilities: Provides extensibility points for custom actions and integrations. Enables triggering of external processes or custom logic at key points in the revision control workflow (e.g., pre-commit checks, post-checkout actions).
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - Extension point; changes could affect integration capabilities but isolated from core logic

key_components.responsibilities: Generates managerial and operational reports. Provides visibility into change history, developer activity, release status, and other metrics supporting the managerial functions mentioned in business goals.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Low - Primarily read-only operations; changes affect reporting capabilities but not core version control

key_components.responsibilities: Manages user interface controls and input handling. Processes user commands, validates inputs, and translates user actions into system operations. May also handle configuration and system settings.
key_components.change_sensitivity: Medium - User-facing component; changes affect usability but can be modified without impacting core logic

architectures.data_flow: User interactions flow through 'ctrls' (Control Interface) to 'main' (Main Controller), which orchestrates operations. For version control operations, 'main' delegates to 'wrcs' (Core Engine), which may invoke 'diff' (Comparison Engine) for change analysis and 'hook' for extensibility triggers. When users request file comparisons, the flow goes: ctrls → main → diff → visdiff for display. Reporting requests flow: ctrls → main → report → wrcs (for data retrieval). Data is primarily file-based (RCS archives) with components passing file paths and version identifiers. The architecture follows a call-and-return pattern with synchronous, tightly-coupled interactions.
architectural_constraints: Monolithic deployment - all components must be deployed together as a single executable unit
Tight coupling through direct function calls limits independent component evolution
RCS-based foundation constrains the underlying version control model and storage format
Reverse-engineered structure suggests limited documentation and potential technical debt
Synchronous processing model - no apparent support for asynchronous or distributed operations
Single-user or sequential access patterns - architecture doesn't indicate concurrent user support
File-system dependent - relies on local file system access for archive storage
architectures.notes:
WHAT THIS ARCHITECTURE CAN EASILY ACCOMMODATE: (1) UI improvements to visdiff and ctrls without affecting core logic; (2) New report types through the reporting module; (3) Additional hooks for workflow customization; (4) Enhanced comparison algorithms in the diff component; (5) Incremental improvements to existing workflows.

WHAT THIS ARCHITECTURE CANNOT EASILY ACCOMMODATE: (1) Distributed or cloud-based operations - monolithic structure assumes local deployment; (2) Concurrent multi-user access - no apparent concurrency control layer; (3) Integration with modern CI/CD pipelines - limited API or service interfaces; (4) Scalability to large teams or repositories - monolithic design limits horizontal scaling; (5) Migration away from RCS - core engine tightly coupled to RCS model; (6) Real-time collaboration features - synchronous, single-user design; (7) Microservices decomposition - tight coupling makes separation difficult.

CHANGE IMPACT ASSESSMENT: Changes to 'main' or 'wrcs' are HIGH RISK and require extensive testing. Changes to 'diff', 'hook', or 'ctrls' are MEDIUM RISK with moderate testing scope. Changes to 'visdiff' or 'report' are LOW RISK and relatively isolated. The reverse-engineered nature means architectural assumptions may not be fully documented, increasing risk for any structural changes.

scenario_elicitation.elicitation_method: AI-Driven Brainstorming based on System Context

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer checks out a source file, modifies it, and checks it back in. The system must record the change, update version history, and complete the operation within 2 seconds for files up to 10MB.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - this is core WRCS functionality. The monolithic architecture with direct function calls supports fast execution. Current architecture should handle this efficiently.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Fundamental workflow for developers using revision control. Fast check-in/check-out is critical for developer productivity and adoption.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user needs to compare two versions of a configuration file and view differences highlighted side-by-side with color coding to identify what changed between plant maintenance procedures from last month versus current version.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - comparison and visualization are existing components. The Visual Difference Display component suggests this capability exists, though quality/usability may vary.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Critical for plant maintenance optimization and safety - users need to understand what changed in procedures to ensure compliance and prevent errors.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator needs to back up the entire WRCS repository containing 5 years of plant simulation data (approximately 500GB) to an offsite location and verify backup integrity within a 4-hour maintenance window.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Partially supported - WRCS has backup functionality mentioned, but the monolithic architecture may not expose APIs for automated backup integration. May require manual processes or custom scripting.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Essential for disaster recovery and regulatory compliance in plant operations. Loss of simulation work history would be catastrophic for safety analysis and auditing.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer needs to diagnose why a specific file checkout failed by examining system logs that show timestamp, user, file path, and error code, with logs retained for 90 days and searchable within 10 seconds.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match - Reporting Module exists but scope unclear. The monolithic architecture may have limited logging infrastructure. Would likely require enhancement to existing Reporting Module and potentially adding logging throughout Core WRCS Engine.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Necessary for troubleshooting and maintaining system reliability. Poor diagnostics lead to extended downtime affecting plant operations and simulation work quality.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer needs to create a release branch for the Q4 plant simulation models, tag it as 'Q4-2024-Release', and ensure all 47 related files are locked from further modification while allowing continued development on the main branch.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - release creation is mentioned as core functionality. However, the extent of branch management and file locking capabilities in the RCS-based system may be limited compared to modern VCS.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Critical for managing simulation work quality and ensuring stable releases for plant operations while allowing ongoing development.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user needs to roll back a plant configuration file to the version from 3 months ago after discovering that recent changes caused simulation errors, completing the rollback within 1 minute without affecting other files.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Direct match - backing up to old versions is explicitly mentioned functionality. RCS-based architecture supports this well through version history.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Essential for plant safety and simulation work efficiency. Ability to quickly recover from errors prevents downtime and maintains operational continuity.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator needs to integrate WRCS with the plant's Active Directory system so that 200 users can authenticate using their existing corporate credentials instead of maintaining separate WRCS passwords.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Indirect match - Extension Hook System suggests some extensibility, but the monolithic architecture with tight coupling makes integration challenging. Would require significant modification to authentication layer, which may be embedded throughout the system.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Improves security and reduces administrative overhead. Single sign-on is increasingly required for enterprise compliance and user convenience.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer needs to upgrade the Comparison Engine to support a new file format (JSON) used in modern plant simulation tools, adding the capability without requiring recompilation of the entire WRCS system.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Challenging - The monolithic architecture with direct function calls and tight coupling suggests that component modifications require full system recompilation. Extension Hook System may provide some plugin capability, but scope is unclear from reverse-engineered architecture.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Supports evolution of simulation work tools and formats. Inability to extend comparison capabilities limits system usefulness as plant technology modernizes.

scenarios.scenario_text: A developer needs to generate a report showing all changes made to safety-critical files in the last 6 months, including who made changes, when, and what was modified, with output in PDF format for regulatory audit.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Partially supported - Reporting Module exists and managerial functions are mentioned, but extent of audit reporting is unclear. May require enhancement to query capabilities and report formatting. PDF generation likely not native.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Critical for regulatory compliance in plant operations. Audit trails are mandatory for safety-critical systems and demonstrate due diligence.

scenarios.scenario_text: An end-user working remotely needs to access WRCS through a web browser to review file history and download specific versions without installing the full WRCS client application.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Major architectural change required - The monolithic executable structure with tight coupling is incompatible with web-based access. Would require creating a service layer, API, and web frontend - essentially a re-architecture from client-server to web-based model.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Improves accessibility and supports modern work patterns (remote work, mobile access). However, represents significant investment with uncertain ROI for plant operations context.

scenarios.scenario_text: A maintainer needs to monitor WRCS system performance metrics (CPU usage, memory consumption, active users, repository size) in real-time through a dashboard to proactively identify performance degradation before it impacts plant simulation work.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Requires new capability - The reverse-engineered architecture shows no monitoring infrastructure. Would need to add instrumentation throughout the monolithic system and create a monitoring interface. The tight coupling makes this invasive.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Proactive monitoring prevents downtime and ensures system reliability for critical plant operations. Performance issues can cascade to affect simulation work efficiency.

scenarios.scenario_text: A system administrator needs to configure automated nightly backups of the WRCS repository to three different locations (local NAS, cloud storage, offsite tape) with email notification of success/failure, without manual intervention.
scenarios.feasibility_note: Requires integration capability - Current architecture may support manual backup but likely lacks scheduling, multi-destination, and notification features. Extension Hook System might enable this, but monolithic design suggests limited external integration points.
scenarios.stakeholder_rationale: Automated backup is essential for business continuity and regulatory compliance. Manual processes are error-prone and don't meet modern disaster recovery requirements.

scenario_classification.rationale: This is core WRCS functionality explicitly mentioned in the architecture. The Main Controller orchestrates check-in/check-out operations through the Core WRCS Engine. The monolithic architecture with direct function calls supports fast, synchronous execution. The 2-second performance requirement for 10MB files is reasonable for local file system operations with RCS-based storage.

The architecture explicitly includes a Comparison Engine (diff) and Visual Difference Display (visdiff) component for this exact purpose. The data flow shows: ctrls → main → diff → visdiff. However, the quality of the visualization (color coding, side-by-side display) depends on the implementation maturity of visdiff, which is unclear from the reverse-engineered architecture.

While WRCS has backup functionality mentioned, the scenario requires automated backup to offsite locations with integrity verification within a time window. The monolithic architecture likely lacks APIs for automated backup orchestration, multi-destination support, and integrity verification. The file-system dependent constraint suggests backups may require manual file system operations rather than integrated backup management.

The Reporting Module exists but is described as generating 'managerial and operational reports' - primarily read-only operations on version control data. Comprehensive diagnostic logging with timestamps, error codes, searchable logs, and 90-day retention represents a different capability. The reverse-engineered architecture shows no logging infrastructure, and adding instrumentation throughout the monolithic system would be invasive.

Release creation and tagging are explicitly mentioned as core WRCS functionality. The Core WRCS Engine handles 'creates releases' and the RCS foundation supports version tagging. However, the extent of branch management and file locking capabilities may be limited compared to modern VCS. RCS does support locking mechanisms, so the basic functionality should exist, though the workflow for managing 47 files simultaneously may require multiple operations.

Rollback to previous versions is explicitly mentioned as core WRCS functionality ('manages rollback to previous versions'). The RCS-based architecture inherently supports retrieving any historical version. The 1-minute performance requirement for a single file rollback is reasonable for local file system operations. File isolation (not affecting other files) is a fundamental RCS capability.

While the Extension Hook System suggests some extensibility, enterprise authentication integration requires changes to the authentication layer, which is likely embedded throughout the monolithic system. The tight coupling and lack of clear security/authentication component boundaries make this challenging. Active Directory integration requires external API calls, configuration management, and potentially session management - capabilities not evident in the current architecture.

The monolithic architecture with tight coupling through direct function calls strongly suggests that component modifications require full system recompilation. The requirement to add JSON support 'without requiring recompilation of the entire WRCS system' conflicts with the architectural constraint of monolithic deployment. While the Extension Hook System might provide some plugin capability, its scope is unclear, and the Comparison Engine has no dependencies, suggesting it's not designed for runtime extensibility.

The Reporting Module exists and supports managerial functions, but generating audit reports with detailed change tracking, user attribution, and PDF output represents enhanced capabilities. The module would need to query detailed change history from Core WRCS Engine, potentially use Comparison Engine for change details, and add PDF generation (likely not native). The scope of current reporting capabilities is unclear from the reverse-engineered architecture.

This represents a fundamental architectural mismatch. The monolithic executable structure with tight coupling is incompatible with web-based access. The architecture explicitly notes it 'CANNOT EASILY ACCOMMODATE: Integration with modern CI/CD pipelines - limited API or service interfaces' and 'Distributed or cloud-based operations - monolithic structure assumes local deployment'. Would require creating a service layer, REST API, web frontend, and authentication system - essentially a complete re-architecture.

The reverse-engineered architecture shows no monitoring or instrumentation infrastructure. Real-time performance monitoring requires adding instrumentation throughout the monolithic system (Main Controller, Core WRCS Engine, potentially all components), collecting metrics, and creating a monitoring interface/dashboard. The tight coupling makes adding cross-cutting concerns like monitoring invasive. The synchronous processing model may not support real-time metric collection without performance impact.

The current architecture may support manual backup but lacks the automation infrastructure for scheduling, multi-destination backup, cloud integration, and notification. The file-system dependent constraint and monolithic design suggest limited external integration points. The Extension Hook System might enable some automation, but coordinating backups to three different destinations (NAS, cloud, tape) with different protocols requires significant integration capability not evident in the architecture.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. This is core functionality already supported by the architecture.
No architectural changes required. May need UI enhancements to visdiff for color coding and side-by-side display if not already present, but the component structure supports this.
issues_identified: Quality of visualization (color coding, side-by-side display) depends on visdiff implementation maturity, which is unclear from reverse-engineered architecture
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: If visdiff lacks advanced visualization, could enhance it (Low risk change per architecture notes) or export diff output to external visualization tools

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add automated backup orchestration capability: (1) Create backup scheduling mechanism in Main Controller or as external script; (2) Extend Core WRCS Engine to expose backup API or ensure RCS archives are accessible for file-system backup; (3) Implement multi-destination backup logic (NAS, offsite) using Extension Hook System or external tools; (4) Add integrity verification (checksum validation) post-backup; (5) Implement time-window monitoring to ensure 4-hour completion.
issues_identified: Monolithic architecture lacks API for automated backup integration
File-system dependent design may require direct file system access rather than integrated backup
500GB data volume requires efficient backup mechanism - network bandwidth and storage I/O may be bottlenecks
Integrity verification not mentioned in current architecture capabilities
No scheduling infrastructure evident in architecture
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use external backup tools (rsync, enterprise backup software) to backup RCS archive directories at file-system level. Use Extension Hook System to trigger external backup scripts. Implement backup orchestration outside WRCS using OS-level scheduling (cron, Task Scheduler) and backup utilities, treating WRCS as a black box with file-system accessible data.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement comprehensive logging infrastructure: (1) Add logging instrumentation throughout Core WRCS Engine to capture operations, errors, user actions, timestamps; (2) Create log storage mechanism with 90-day retention policy; (3) Extend Reporting Module to provide log search and query capabilities with <10 second response time; (4) Add structured logging format to capture timestamp, user, file path, error codes; (5) Implement log rotation and archival.
issues_identified: No logging infrastructure evident in reverse-engineered architecture
Adding logging throughout monolithic system is invasive and affects multiple components
Log search performance (<10 seconds) may require indexing infrastructure not present
90-day retention requires storage management and archival strategy
Changes to Core WRCS Engine are HIGH RISK per architecture notes
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement logging at OS/file-system level using system audit tools (auditd on Linux, File Auditing on Windows) to track file access and modifications. Use Extension Hook System to log key operations at integration points. Implement application-level logging in Main Controller and Control Interface (lower risk components) to capture user actions, then correlate with system logs. Use external log aggregation tools (syslog, ELK stack) for search and retention.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. Release creation and tagging are core functionality. May need to execute multiple operations to tag and lock 47 files, but capability exists.
issues_identified: RCS-based branching may be less sophisticated than modern VCS (Git) - may require workarounds for branch management
Managing 47 files simultaneously may require scripting or batch operations rather than single command
File locking mechanism extent unclear - may lock entire files rather than support concurrent development on different branches
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: If batch operations not supported, create scripts using Main Controller to iterate through 47 files for tagging and locking. Use Extension Hook System to automate multi-file release creation workflow.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: No changes required. Rollback to previous versions is core functionality explicitly supported.

Implement Active Directory integration: (1) Add authentication abstraction layer in Control Interface to separate authentication from authorization; (2) Implement AD/LDAP client library integration for credential validation; (3) Modify Main Controller to support external authentication flow; (4) Add configuration management for AD connection settings (server, domain, SSL); (5) Implement session management for authenticated users; (6) Add fallback authentication for AD unavailability; (7) Map AD users/groups to WRCS permissions.
issues_identified: Authentication layer likely embedded throughout monolithic system - no clear security component boundary
Tight coupling makes adding authentication abstraction layer difficult
Changes to Main Controller are HIGH RISK per architecture notes
No evidence of external integration capabilities beyond Extension Hook System
Session management may not exist in current architecture
User management and permissions model unclear from reverse-engineered architecture
AD integration requires external library dependencies and network communication
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use OS-level authentication - run WRCS as authenticated user and rely on file-system permissions for access control. Implement authentication proxy/wrapper that validates AD credentials before launching WRCS session. Use Extension Hook System to call external AD validation script before allowing operations. Implement lightweight authentication gateway that validates credentials then passes control to WRCS with validated user context.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Add JSON comparison capability: (1) Extend Comparison Engine to detect file type and route to appropriate comparison algorithm; (2) Implement JSON-aware comparison logic (structure-aware diff, not just text diff); (3) Add plugin architecture to Comparison Engine to support runtime format extensions; (4) Modify Core WRCS Engine to pass file type metadata to Comparison Engine; (5) Update Visual Difference Display to render JSON differences appropriately. Note: Avoiding recompilation requires plugin architecture, which conflicts with monolithic design.
issues_identified: Monolithic architecture requires full system recompilation for component changes
Comparison Engine has no dependencies, suggesting it's not designed for extensibility
Adding plugin architecture conflicts with tight coupling architectural constraint
Extension Hook System scope unclear - may not support runtime component extension
Changes to Comparison Engine are MEDIUM RISK but adding plugin architecture affects Core WRCS Engine (HIGH RISK)
Runtime extensibility requires dynamic loading mechanisms not evident in architecture
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Accept recompilation requirement and add JSON support directly to Comparison Engine (simpler, aligns with architecture). Use Extension Hook System to call external JSON comparison tool and integrate results. Implement file type detection in Control Interface and route JSON files to external comparison tool, bypassing internal Comparison Engine. Create wrapper scripts that pre-process JSON files to normalized text format before comparison.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Enhance audit reporting capability: (1) Extend Reporting Module to query detailed change history from Core WRCS Engine including user, timestamp, file path, change description; (2) Add filtering capability for safety-critical files and 6-month time window; (3) Implement change detail extraction using Comparison Engine to show what was modified; (4) Add PDF generation library and formatting logic to Reporting Module; (5) Create audit report template with regulatory compliance formatting; (6) Add report scheduling and export capabilities.
issues_identified: Current Reporting Module scope unclear - may lack detailed change tracking queries
PDF generation likely not native - requires external library integration
Identifying 'safety-critical files' requires metadata/tagging not evident in architecture
Detailed change extraction may require multiple queries to Core WRCS Engine and Comparison Engine
Report formatting for regulatory compliance may require specific templates and standards
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Export raw data from Reporting Module to CSV/XML and use external tools (Excel, reporting software) for PDF generation and formatting. Use Extension Hook System to trigger external reporting tools that query RCS archives directly. Implement report generation as external script that accesses WRCS data through file system and generates PDF using standard libraries. Create simple text/HTML reports from Reporting Module and convert to PDF using OS utilities (wkhtmltopdf, print-to-PDF).

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Re-architect for web access: (1) Create service/API layer to expose Core WRCS Engine functionality via REST/HTTP; (2) Implement authentication and session management for web access; (3) Develop web frontend for file browsing, history viewing, and version download; (4) Adapt Visual Difference Display for web rendering (HTML/JavaScript); (5) Implement security controls for remote access; (6) Add web server infrastructure; (7) Refactor Control Interface to support both desktop and web clients. This is essentially a complete re-architecture from monolithic desktop to client-server web model.
issues_identified: Fundamental architectural mismatch - monolithic executable incompatible with web access
Architecture explicitly notes it CANNOT EASILY ACCOMMODATE distributed/cloud operations and modern integrations
Tight coupling prevents clean separation of business logic from presentation
No API or service layer exists
Security model for remote access not present
Would require changes to all components - essentially a re-architecture
Changes to Main Controller and Core WRCS Engine are HIGH RISK
Synchronous processing model may not scale for concurrent web users
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement read-only web interface that accesses RCS archives at file-system level, bypassing WRCS application (limited functionality but lower risk). Create web gateway that executes WRCS commands via command-line interface and returns results (security concerns). Use remote desktop/VNC to provide web-based access to full WRCS desktop application (poor UX but no architectural changes). Implement file sharing of RCS archives with web-based RCS viewer (separate tool). Consider this a future-state architecture goal and maintain current desktop-only access.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement performance monitoring: (1) Add instrumentation throughout Main Controller, Core WRCS Engine, and other components to collect metrics (CPU, memory, operation counts, timing); (2) Create metrics collection and aggregation mechanism; (3) Implement metrics storage with time-series data; (4) Extend Reporting Module or create new monitoring component to expose metrics via dashboard; (5) Add real-time metric streaming capability; (6) Implement alerting for performance thresholds; (7) Create monitoring dashboard UI (web or desktop).
issues_identified: No monitoring infrastructure evident in reverse-engineered architecture
Adding instrumentation throughout monolithic system is invasive cross-cutting concern
Changes to Main Controller and Core WRCS Engine are HIGH RISK
Real-time monitoring may impact performance of synchronous processing model
Dashboard requires new UI component or web interface (see S-10 issues)
Tight coupling makes adding monitoring hooks difficult without affecting core logic
Metrics collection and storage requires new infrastructure components
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Use OS-level monitoring tools (Task Manager, top, Performance Monitor) to track CPU/memory without application changes. Implement external monitoring that observes WRCS process metrics via OS APIs. Add lightweight logging to Extension Hook System for operation tracking and analyze logs for performance trends (not real-time). Use file-system monitoring to track repository size growth. Implement monitoring wrapper that launches WRCS and collects process-level metrics. Accept that detailed internal monitoring is not feasible with current architecture and rely on external/OS-level tools.

scenario_evaluations.change_description: Implement automated multi-destination backup: (1) Create backup orchestration component or extend Main Controller with scheduling capability; (2) Implement backup adapters for each destination type (NAS - file copy, Cloud - API integration, Tape - backup software integration); (3) Add configuration management for backup destinations, credentials, schedules; (4) Implement backup verification and integrity checking; (5) Add email notification system for success/failure; (6) Create backup scheduling mechanism (cron integration or internal scheduler); (7) Use Extension Hook System to trigger backup workflow; (8) Implement backup status tracking and logging.
issues_identified: No scheduling infrastructure evident in architecture
Multi-destination backup requires integration with diverse systems (NAS, cloud APIs, tape software)
Cloud integration requires external API libraries and authentication
Email notification requires SMTP integration not present
Monolithic design limits external integration points beyond Extension Hook System
Backup verification requires checksum/integrity mechanisms not mentioned
Configuration management for multiple destinations adds complexity
File-system dependent architecture may complicate cloud backup
scenario_evaluations.alternative_approaches: Implement backup orchestration entirely outside WRCS using OS-level tools: (1) Use cron/Task Scheduler for scheduling; (2) Use rsync/robocopy for NAS backup; (3) Use cloud CLI tools (aws s3, rclone) for cloud backup; (4) Use enterprise backup software for tape; (5) Use shell scripts to orchestrate and verify; (6) Use mail command/PowerShell for notifications. Treat WRCS as black box and backup RCS archive directories at file-system level. Use Extension Hook System only to ensure WRCS is in consistent state before backup (quiesce operations). This approach requires no WRCS architectural changes and leverages mature external tools.

scenario_interaction_weights.rationale: Core WRCS Engine is the architectural heart with 7 scenario dependencies. Changes here are HIGH RISK per architecture notes. Affects critical business continuity (S-03: 500GB backup), compliance (S-04: audit logging, S-09: regulatory reports), and strategic modernization (S-10: web access, S-11: monitoring). Failure to address backup/logging scenarios creates operational and regulatory risk. The monolithic design makes this component a bottleneck - any invasive changes risk system stability. All stakeholder roles depend on core engine reliability.

Main Controller orchestrates 6 scenarios including high-risk architectural changes (S-07: AD integration, S-10: web re-architecture, S-11: performance monitoring). This component is HIGH RISK for changes and serves as integration point for enterprise requirements. S-07 affects 1,200 users' authentication experience. S-10 represents fundamental architectural shift that stakeholders desire but architecture cannot easily support. Failure impacts security posture, scalability, and operational visibility. Primarily affects administrators managing the system and developers/maintainers implementing changes.

Extension Hook System appears in 4 scenarios as the primary architectural escape hatch for external integrations. Critical for implementing workarounds to monolithic limitations (S-03: backup orchestration, S-07: AD authentication wrapper, S-08: external JSON comparison, S-12: backup triggers). This component's extensibility determines feasibility of alternative approaches that avoid high-risk core changes. However, its scope and capabilities are unclear from reverse-engineered architecture. Medium priority because it enables risk mitigation strategies but isn't directly user-facing. Impacts technical staff implementing integrations.

Reporting Module supports 3 scenarios with compliance and operational visibility focus. S-04 (audit logging with <10 sec search) and S-09 (regulatory audit reports) have direct compliance implications for safety-critical aerospace environment. S-11 (performance monitoring dashboard) affects operational management. Reporting is MEDIUM RISK for changes per architecture notes. Failure to enhance reporting creates compliance risk and operational blind spots but doesn't impact core version control functionality. Primarily affects administrators needing visibility and end-users requiring audit evidence.

Control Interface involved in 2 scenarios representing user interaction layer changes. S-07 (AD integration) requires authentication abstraction affecting all 1,200 users' login experience. S-10 (web access) requires fundamental UI re-architecture from desktop to web model. While only 2 scenarios, both represent significant user experience shifts and architectural challenges. Medium priority because changes here cascade to other components but Control Interface itself is lower risk than Main Controller. Impacts end-users' daily interaction patterns and developers implementing UI changes.

Comparison Engine appears in only 2 scenarios with limited business impact. S-08 (JSON comparison) is a feature enhancement for specific file types, not core functionality. S-09 (audit reports) uses diff for change details but has alternative approaches. Changes are MEDIUM RISK and localized. While JSON support would improve developer experience for configuration files, lack of this feature doesn't create operational or compliance risk. Lowest priority as it represents nice-to-have enhancements rather than critical business needs. Primarily affects developers working with JSON files and end-users viewing detailed change reports.

architectural_strengths: Strong core version control functionality: Architecture directly supports fundamental version control scenarios (S-01: basic operations, S-05: release management, S-06: rollback) with no changes required. The RCS-based Core WRCS Engine provides proven, stable version control capabilities that meet primary business needs.

Low-risk visualization and reporting layer: The Reporting Module and Visual Difference Display components are explicitly noted as LOW RISK for modifications, enabling safe enhancements for user-facing features like improved diff visualization (S-02) and audit reporting (S-09) without jeopardizing system stability.

Extension Hook System provides integration escape hatch: Present in 4 scenarios (S-03, S-07, S-08, S-12) as the primary mechanism for external integrations. This component enables workarounds to monolithic limitations by allowing external tools and scripts to integrate without invasive core changes, critical for backup orchestration and authentication wrappers.

File-system based architecture enables external tooling: The file-system dependent design, while limiting in some contexts, allows alternative approaches using mature OS-level tools (rsync, cron, audit tools) for backup (S-03, S-12), logging (S-04), and monitoring (S-11) without requiring application changes.

Proven stability for safety-critical environment: The monolithic, tightly-coupled design that resists change also provides stability and predictability valued in aerospace safety-critical contexts. Direct support for core scenarios with LOW RISK indicates reliable existing functionality.
architectural_weaknesses: Monolithic architecture prevents modular evolution: Tight coupling and lack of component boundaries make 8 of 12 scenarios require Indirect/Widespread changes. The architecture 'CANNOT EASILY ACCOMMODATE distributed/cloud operations and modern integrations' (S-10), fundamentally limiting strategic modernization. Changes require full system recompilation, affecting development velocity and deployment flexibility.

High-risk core components are architectural hotspots: Core WRCS Engine (7 scenarios) and Main Controller (6 scenarios) are both HIGH RISK for changes yet required for critical business needs including backup (S-03), logging (S-04), AD integration (S-07), web access (S-10), and monitoring (S-11). This creates a dangerous bottleneck where business-critical enhancements risk system stability.

Missing enterprise integration capabilities: No native support for authentication abstraction (S-07: 1,200 users need AD), API/service layer (S-10: web access), monitoring infrastructure (S-11), or scheduling mechanisms (S-03, S-12). The architecture lacks the integration points expected in modern enterprise environments, forcing invasive changes or external workarounds.

Inadequate observability and operational tooling: No logging infrastructure (S-04), performance monitoring (S-11), or comprehensive audit capabilities (S-09) evident in the architecture. For a system managing 500GB of safety-critical aerospace data with regulatory compliance requirements, the lack of operational visibility creates compliance and troubleshooting risks.

Extensibility conflicts with architectural constraints: S-08 (JSON comparison) exposes fundamental tension - adding plugin architecture for runtime extensibility conflicts with monolithic design requiring recompilation. The Comparison Engine has 'no dependencies, suggesting it's not designed for extensibility,' limiting ability to support new file formats without invasive changes.

Synchronous processing model limits scalability: Architecture designed for single-user desktop usage struggles with concurrent access scenarios (S-10: web access for multiple users, S-11: real-time monitoring). The synchronous processing model and lack of session management prevent scaling to modern multi-user, distributed usage patterns.
overall_results.modifiability_assessment: The architecture exhibits LOW modifiability with significant technical debt. While core version control functions are stable and directly supported, 67% of scenarios (8 of 12) require Indirect changes with Medium-to-High effort, and 50% are High risk. The monolithic, tightly-coupled design creates a brittle system where business-critical enhancements (enterprise integration, observability, modernization) require invasive changes to high-risk components, fundamentally limiting evolutionary capacity. The architecture is optimized for stability over adaptability, creating a strategic liability as stakeholder needs evolve toward modern enterprise capabilities.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement comprehensive external integration strategy using Extension Hook System and OS-level tools rather than invasive core changes. For S-03/S-12 (backup), S-04 (logging), and S-11 (monitoring), leverage file-system access, cron/Task Scheduler, rsync, system audit tools, and external monitoring rather than modifying Core WRCS Engine or Main Controller. Document and standardize these integration patterns.
recommendations.rationale: Avoids HIGH RISK changes to core components (wrcs, main) while addressing 5 critical scenarios. Leverages existing architectural strength (file-system based design) and Extension Hook System. Provides immediate operational capabilities (backup, logging, monitoring) needed for 500GB safety-critical environment without jeopardizing system stability. This is the only feasible near-term approach given architectural constraints.

recommendations.recommendation: Create authentication wrapper/proxy for AD integration (S-07) rather than embedding authentication in Main Controller. Implement OS-level or gateway-based authentication that validates credentials before launching WRCS session, mapping AD users to file-system permissions. Use Extension Hook System to enforce authentication checks.
recommendations.rationale: Addresses critical security requirement for 1,200 users while avoiding HIGH RISK changes to Main Controller and embedded authentication logic. Separates authentication concern from core application, improving security posture without invasive refactoring. Enables centralized credential management and audit trail. This approach is architecturally aligned with external integration strategy and provides path to future service-oriented architecture.

recommendations.recommendation: Establish architectural roadmap for gradual service-oriented migration. Phase 1: Extract reporting and monitoring as separate services accessing RCS archives (addresses S-09, S-11). Phase 2: Create read-only API layer for web access (S-10). Phase 3: Migrate write operations to service layer. Maintain existing desktop application during transition. Set 18-24 month timeline with stakeholder alignment.
recommendations.rationale: S-10 (web access) represents fundamental architectural mismatch that cannot be addressed with current monolithic design. Rather than attempting invasive re-architecture, establish incremental migration path that delivers value progressively while managing risk. Reporting and monitoring are LOW/MEDIUM RISK components suitable for early extraction. This addresses strategic modernization need while preserving stability of core version control. Aligns with industry patterns for legacy system evolution.

recommendations.recommendation: Enhance Reporting Module with audit and compliance capabilities (S-04, S-09) including structured logging queries, PDF generation via external libraries, safety-critical file tagging, and 90-day retention. This is a MEDIUM RISK, localized change that delivers high compliance value.
recommendations.rationale: Reporting Module is LOW RISK for changes and addresses 2 compliance-critical scenarios in safety-critical aerospace environment. Regulatory audit reports (S-09) and searchable audit logs (S-04) are non-negotiable for compliance. This is a 'quick win' that delivers business value without touching high-risk core components. Can be implemented independently and provides foundation for future service extraction (supports High priority roadmap recommendation).

recommendations.recommendation: Accept recompilation requirement and add JSON comparison directly to Comparison Engine (S-08) rather than attempting plugin architecture. Implement file type detection and structure-aware diff for JSON, XML, and other common configuration formats as native capability.
recommendations.rationale: S-08 exposed that plugin architecture conflicts with monolithic design constraints. Rather than fighting the architecture, work with it - add format support directly as MEDIUM RISK localized change to Comparison Engine. This is architecturally aligned, provides developer value for configuration file management, and avoids complexity of runtime extensibility that the architecture cannot support. Simpler implementation with lower risk than plugin approach.

recommendations.recommendation: Implement batch operation scripting framework using Main Controller for multi-file operations (S-05: 47 files). Create command-line interface and scripting examples for common workflows (release creation, bulk tagging, batch rollback). Document Extension Hook System capabilities and provide integration templates.
recommendations.rationale: S-05 identified potential gap in batch operations for release management. While core capability exists, usability for large file sets may require scripting. This recommendation improves operational efficiency without architectural changes. Additionally, documenting Extension Hook System addresses uncertainty about its scope and capabilities, enabling better use of this critical integration point across multiple scenarios. Low effort, high operational value.

recommendations.recommendation: Defer web-based UI re-architecture (S-10) until service-oriented migration (High priority roadmap) reaches Phase 2. In interim, provide remote desktop/VNC access for remote users if web access is critical business need. Focus architectural investment on higher-priority integration and compliance scenarios.
recommendations.rationale: S-10 requires fundamental re-architecture that is HIGH RISK, HIGH EFFORT, and conflicts with core architectural constraints. The evaluation correctly identifies this as 'essentially a complete re-architecture from monolithic desktop to client-server web model.' Rather than attempting this in current architecture, defer until service layer exists (per roadmap recommendation). Remote desktop provides interim solution with no architectural changes. This prioritization focuses limited resources on achievable, high-value improvements.

recommendations.recommendation: Establish architectural governance and change risk assessment process. Document component risk levels (HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW), require architectural review for changes to Core WRCS Engine and Main Controller, and mandate alternative analysis for high-risk changes. Create architectural decision records (ADRs) for significant modifications.
recommendations.rationale: The architecture's tight coupling and high-risk core components require disciplined change management to prevent stability degradation. Multiple scenarios (S-04, S-07, S-10, S-11) identified HIGH RISK changes to wrcs and main components. Formal governance ensures risk-aware decision making, encourages external integration approaches over invasive changes, and maintains architectural knowledge. This is foundational for managing technical debt and supporting the service-oriented migration roadmap.
